title: W02.Deck.fins3646
body:
FINS3646: Toolkit for Finance Week 2: Lecture

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin UNSW

Overview

Objects Basic types Assignment statements Names Downloading stock prices

Important

This week will introduce some concepts that will be explored in greater depth later in the course. I’ll point these out as we progress.

Objects

A class is a blueprint that defines the structure and behaviour of its instances. Classes

A class is a blueprint that defines the structure and behaviour of its instances. Classes An instance is a specific object created from a class.  Each instance shares the same behaviour as the class but can hold its own data.

A class is a blueprint that defines the structure and behaviour of its instances. Classes An instance is a specific object created from a class.  Each instance shares the same behaviour as the class but can hold its own data. An object is an instance of a class stored in computer memory

A class is a blueprint that defines the structure and behaviour of its instances. Classes An instance is a specific object created from a class.  Each instance shares the same behaviour as the class but can hold its own data. An object is an instance of a class stored in computer memory

In this course, we’ll use the terms object and instance interchangeably.

Python: Creating instances with constructors and literals  Review: Objects in Python

Python is an object-oriented language  Everything in Python is an object (an instance of some class) A class is a blueprint for objects  Calling a class constructs an instance  The class defines the attributes and methods shared by its instances Every instance has exactly one class  That class is the instance’s type For our purposes, an instance’s type is simply its class (the one you called).

Python: Creating instances with constructors and literals

Python uses the class as a template to create instances.

You can always create an instance with the class name and parameter values in parentheses. Examples include: int(1), for integers float(1.0), for non-integer real numbers str('This is text'), for text

This approach uses constructors.

Python: Creating instances with constructors and literals

Python uses the class as a template to create instances.

Fundamental classes allow you to create instances with a shorthand that requires just specifying a value. For example, 1 (creates the same object as int(1)) 1.0 (creates the same object as float(1.0)) 'This is text' (creates the same object as str('This is text'))

This approach uses literals.

Python: Creating instances with constructors and literals

Why use literals?

They are easier to read, cleaner, and shorter Python optimises literals so they are typically faster than constructors

Why use constructors?

Most of the time, you must. Every class has a constructor, while only a select few classes have literals. The resulting class is very explicit. They are not susceptible to minor typographical errors (e.g., whole number integers and floating points)

In general, use a literal instead of the constructor whenever possible.

Python: Basic building blocks  Basic building blocks

Five of Python’s essential types:

Four primitive types

int: Integers representing whole numbers exactly float: Floating points representing real numbers inexactly str: Strings of characters representing textual information bool: Boolean values representing logical True/False

One special ‘singleton’ type

NoneType: None, a single instance representing missing information

Whole numbers: Integer

Integers are whole-valued numerical data (positive, zero, and negative).

Instances are created by: A sequence of digits without a decimal point:  Python recognises 1 as an integer.  Python considers 1. and 1.0 to be floating-point numbers. A preceding negative sign indicates a negative value: Underscores may be used to group digits:  Python recognises 1_000 as an integer Commas may not be used to group digits:  Python interprets 1,000 to be a tuple (to be discussed later)

Integers are represented as instances of the int class

Real numbers: Floating point

Floating point numbers (floats) are inexact, binary representations of real-valued numbers.

Instances are created by: Two methods:  A sequence of digits followed by a decimal point with optional additional digits after the decimal point. • Python considers 1. and 1.0 to be floating-point numbers. • Python considers 1.1 to be a floating-point number.  A decimal point followed by digits. A preceding negative sign indicates a negative value: Underscores may be used to group digits:  Python recognises 1_000.1 as an integer Commas may not be used to group digits:  Python interprets 1,000.1 to be a tuple (to be discussed later)

Floating point numbers are represented as instances of the float class

Textual information: String

Strings are textual data.

Instances are created by: Text on a single line surrounded by single- or double-quotes. Text on a single or multiple line surrounded by three repeated single- or double-quotes. Use these only when appropriate. The symbol used to start the string must be used to end it. Examples: string_a = "This is a my favourite sentence." string_b = 'This is a my favourite sentence.' string_c = """This is my favourite sentence. I have so much to say that my thoughts go on to a second line. Let me tell you about the time ...""" string_d = '''This is my favourite sentence. I have so much to say that my thoughts go on to a second line. Let me tell you about the time ...'''

Strings are represented as instances of the str class

Textual information: String

Note that quotation characters can be included by either:

Starting the string with a different quotation character, e.g., starting a string with double-quotes, will let you have single-quotes inside it. "When Cleese says: 'Now that's what I call a dead parrot'"

Preceding (escaping) the quotation character with a backslash: \" or \' "Narrator: \"And now for something completely different \"" 'Narrator: \'And now for something completely different \''

True/False information: Boolean

Booleans are the logical values of true and false.

The two instances are created by: True False

Capitalisation matters. Python considers lowercase true and false to be variable names.

True and False are the only instances of the bool class

Missing information: None

NoneType is used to represent missing information.

The single instance is created by: The keyword None Capitalisation matters. Python considers none to be a variable name.

None is the only member of Python’s NoneType class

Missing information: None

None is very useful because information is often missing. Note that:

Operations between None and other basic types are not permitted. Method calls with None will yield an error.

None means that something is missing. It does not mean 0, False, or an empty string, which are instances of their respective types.

Creating instances  Constructors: ClassName(...) (always available).  Literals: shorthand for fundamental classes (1, 1.0, 'text', True, None). Essential types  int: Represent whole numbers  float: Represent real numbers with a decimal point; inexact representation.  str: Represent text in quotes ("..." or '...'; triple quotes for multi-line).  bool: Represent exactly True or False (capitalisation matters)  NoneType: None, a single value meaning missing information Pitfalls to remember  None is not 0, False, or ""; don’t do arithmetic or method calls on None  1 is an int, while 1.0 and 1. are float

Once we have created an object in Python, we need a way to identify, refer to, and use it.

Names are an imperfect way to identify instances.

Instances may have multiple names: Germany (an instance of a Country) is also known as Deutschland (German), Allemagne (French), Alemania (Spanish), Saksa (Finnish), among others. Instances may change names: This School (an instance of a University division) has been known as the Faculty of Commerce (1955-1987), the Faculty of Commerce and Economics (1988-2005), the Faculty of Business (2006), the Australian School of Business (2007-2013), and UNSW Business School (2014 - present). Names can change instances: The Prime Minister of Australia (a name) has been Anthony Albanese (2022–present), Scott Morrison (2018–2022), Malcolm Turnbull (2015–2018), Tony Abbott (2013–2015), Julia Gillard (2010–2013), Kevin Rudd (2007–2010; 2013), John Howard (1996–2007), Paul Keating (1991–1996), Bob Hawke (1983–1991), and others.

Names are an imperfect way to identify instances.

Instances may have multiple names: Germany (an instance of a Country) is also known as Deutschland (German), Allemagne (French), Alemania (Spanish), Saksa (Finnish), among others. Instances may change names: This School (an instance of a University division) has been known as the Faculty of Commerce (1955-1987), the Faculty of Commerce and Economics (1988-2005), the Faculty of Business (2006), the Australian School of Business (2007-2013), and UNSW Business School (2014 - present). Names can change instances: The Prime Minister of Australia (a name) has been Anthony Albanese (2022–present), Scott Morrison (2018–2022), Malcolm Turnbull (2015–2018), Tony Abbott (2013–2015), Julia Gillard (2010–2013), Kevin Rudd (2007–2010; 2013), John Howard (1996–2007), Paul Keating (1991–1996), Bob Hawke (1983–1991), and others.

We need unique and permanent identifiers for instances.

In Python, a name is a references to an object stored in memory.

We use names (variables) to refer to objects in the computer memory.

Names reflects real-world scenarios. . .

Instances can have multiple names:  The lecturers_phone is also that_annoying_persons_phone. Instances can change names:  The neighbours_device becomes my_backup_device if your neighbour gives it to you. Names can change instances:  This lecturers_phone is different from the lecturers_phone for a different class. But names do not uniquely identify objects.

To identify an object uniquely, we need to examine its “identity”:

The identity of an object is an integer which is guaranteed to be unique and constant for this object during its lifetime. It is assigned by the Python interpreter when the object is created and remains constant until the object is deleted.

To identify an object uniquely, we need to examine its “identity”:

The identity of an object is an integer which is guaranteed to be unique and constant for this object during its lifetime. It is assigned by the Python interpreter when the object is created and remains constant until the object is deleted.

At a given point in time, two objects have the same identify if, and only if, they are the same object.

We can access the identifier for each object using Python’s id(object) function.

In an interactive Python environment, we can explore the ids of different instances: >>> id(1) 4366313776 >>> id(True) 4376937664

Note: The identifiers you obtain from running the code will differ from those above.

When executing code:

Python will give each instance a unique identifier Python will keep this identifier constant as long as the instance exists

This gives us two important takeaways: 1. We generally shouldn’t use identifiers in our code. Identifiers will differ across computers and can even differ on a single computer each time Python is executed. 2. We can rely on the Python interpreter to give each instance a unique and constant identifier.

We use names as a way to refer to objects:

We use names as a way to refer to objects:

Viewing names as links to unique identifiers reflects real-world scenarios: Instances can have multiple names:  The lecturers_phone Ñ 123456 is also that_annoying_persons_phone Ñ 123456. Instances can change names:  The neighbours_device Ñ 867137 becomes my_backup_device Ñ 867137 if your neighbour gives it to you. Names can change instances:  The lecturers_phone Ñ 123456 will become lecturers_phone Ñ 612584 once you attend a different class.

We use names as a way to refer to objects:

Suppose we have two names x and y:

If id(x) is the same as id(y), then x and y are two names for the same object: x Ñ obj Ð y If id(x) is not the same as id(y), then x and y are names for different objects: x Ñ obj1, y Ñ obj2, obj1  obj2

Recall:

At a given point in time, two objects have the same identify if, and only if, they are the same object.

Recall:

At a given point in time, two objects have the same identify if, and only if, they are the same object.

We can test if two names refer to the same object using: The id() function and boolean equality == (note the double equals sign). id(x) == id(y) -> bool

The is operator: x is y

A value of True means that the names refer to the same object, a value of False means they do not.

Names are imperfect ways to refer to instances. The identifier concept ensures that every instance of every class has a unique identity. Names are then a way to refer to an identifier.  The name of an instance may change – its identifier will not.  A name may change the instance to which it relates. We can write the link as name Ñ obj

Assignment statements  Once we have created an object in Python, we need a way to identify, refer to, and use it.

Assignment statements

We will break the discussion of assignments into a few sub-sections:

Variables and assignment statements Expressions Variable naming

When Python creates an object:

It stores the object somewhere in the computer’s memory. The location of the object is called its memory address. Python can find objects using their memory addresses, but:

Programmers do not know these addresses in advance. Addresses can change each time we run the program.

It is hard to use memory addresses directly in code. . .

A variable is a name in our code that Python links to an object’s memory address while it executes a program. We use variables to store and access information. This information can be an int, str, or any Python object. The object can be used or changed by our code.

We attach a variable to a memory location using an assignment statement

Assignment statements have the form: variable_name = expression

On execution, Python: 1. Checks that variable_name is a valid name 2. Evaluates the expression 3. Assigns the instance produced by the expression to variable_name.

The use of the equals sign in the assignment statement may be confusing to new programmers. variable_name = expression

Read the statement as variable_name is assigned to the result of the expression. Do not interpret this as variable_name is equal to the expression.

We will return to this point later in the lecture.

Basic assignment statement using the primitive types and None are shown below:

x = 5 # int pi_approx = 3.14 # float name = "Alice" # str is_valid = True # bool result = None # NoneType

Let’s pause and summarise what we’ve learned:

Identifiers and names:  Names are imperfect ways to refer to instances.  The identifier concept ensures that every instance of every class has a unique identity.  Names are then a way to refer to an identifier. • The name of an instance may change – its identifier will not. • A name may change the instance to which it relates.  We can write the link as name Ñ obj

Variables and assignment statements:  A variable is a name in our code that Python will link with an instance’s unique identifier as it executes a program.  We attach a variable to a memory location using an assignment statement: variable_name = expression

An expression is a combination of instances, operations, and functions. Python evaluates expressions, producing an instance as the result.

Some expressions using integers are: int(1)

int(1) + int(2)

abs(int(-2))

int(9) ** abs(int(-2))

An expression is a combination of instances, operations, and functions. Python evaluates expressions, producing an instance as the result.

Some expressions using integers are: int(1)

int(1) + int(2)

abs(int(-2))

int(9) ** abs(int(-2))

The examples in this section use int(), float(), and str() constructors instead of literals to draw attention to the fact that Python must create an instance of any object before it can perform any work.

When evaluating int(1), Python will: Create an int instance with a value of 1

An int instance with a value of 1 is the result of this expression.

When evaluating int(1) + int(2), Python will: Create an int instance with a value of 1 Create an int instance with a value of 2 Verify that the int class supports addition Create an int instance with a value of 1 plus 2

An int instance with a value of 3 is the result of this expression.

When evaluating abs(int(-2)), Python will: Create an int instance with a value of -2 Verify that the int class supports the abs absolute value function Create an int instance with a value of the absolute value of -2

An int instance with a value of 2 is the result of this expression.

When evaluating int(9) ** abs(int(-2)), Python will: Create an int instance with a value of 9 Evaluate the abs(int(-2)) sub-expression  Create an int instance with a value of -2  Verify that the int class supports the abs absolute value function  Create an int instance with a value of 2 Verify that the int class supports exponentiation using the ** operator. Create an int instance with the value of 9 raised to the 2nd power.

An int instance with a value of 81 is the result of this expression.

When evaluating float(9) ** abs(int(-2)), Python will: Create a float instance with a value of 9 Evaluate the abs(int(-2)) sub-expression  Create an int instance with a value of -2  Verify that the int class supports the abs absolute value function  Create an int instance with a value of 2 Verify that the float class supports exponentiation to an int power using the ** operator. Create a float instance with the value of 9 raised to the 2nd power.

A float instance with a value of 81.0 is the result of this expression.

A note about mathematical operators for integers and floating-point instances

Most algebraic operators work as expected: Addition: + (e.g., x + y) Subtraction: - (e.g., x - y) Multiplication * (e.g., x * y) Division: / (e.g., x / y)

One common algebraic operator works differently than expected: Exponentiation: **

Python uses two stars (**) for exponentiation. The caret operator (ˆ) has an entirely different meaning.

"Hello" .upper()

When evaluating "Hello".upper(), Python will: Create a str instance with a “Hello” Call the instance method .upper on this instance Receive a new instance from the method with all letters in uppercase

A str instance with a value of “HELLO” is the result of this expression.

(True or False) and False

When evaluating (True or False) and False, Python will: Process the parenthetical phrase  Create a bool instance with the value of True  Create a bool instance with the value of False  Apply the or operator on the previous two instances, yielding True Finalise the expression  Use True as the result of the parenthetical phrase  Recall the bool instance with the value of False  Apply the and operator on the previous two instances, yielding False

A bool instance with a value of False result of this expression.

Identifiers and names:  Names are imperfect ways to refer to instances.  The identifier concept ensures that every instance of every class has a unique identity.  Names are then a way to refer to an identifier. • The name of an instance may change – its identifier will not. • A name may change the instance to which it relates.  We can write the link as name Ñ obj

Variables and assignment statements:  A variable is a name in our code that Python will link with an instance’s unique identifier as it executes a program.  We attach a variable to a memory location using an assignment statement: variable_name = expression

Expressions:  An expression is a combination of instances, operations, and functions.  Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances, producing an instance as the result.

Python: An aside about rules and conventions  Rules and conventions

Rules and conventions are important to follow when writing code:

Rules: Define what you are allowed to do Conventions: Define what you should do and, more importantly, what others expect you to have done.

The Python community treats conventions seriously.

“PEP 8 - Style Guide for Python Code” is an essential reference for Python coding conventions.

A consistent, disciplined approach that follows established rules and conventions will help you and others understand and maintain your code effectively.

Rules: Naming

Naming rules apply to any variable or function we create.

Names must start with a letter or underscore (“_“). Names can only contain alphanumeric characters and underscores. Names are case-sensitive.

As long as you follow these rules, you can use nearly anything as a name, however. . .

The Python community has settled on snake_case as the de facto standard when naming variables:

The Python community has settled on snake_case as the de facto standard when naming variables:

It is best to be expressive when naming variables, clearly describing the information to which the variable relates.

For example, when naming a variable reflecting the price of a call option: Best: call_option_price Bad: calloptionprice Worse: cop

Python: Variable names - Rule (Reserved keywords)

Python reserved keywords cannot be used for variable or function names. These include:

False await else import pass None break except in raise True class finally is return and continue for lambda try as def from nonlocal while assert del global not with async elif if or yield

Using a reserved keyword as a variable name results in a syntax error.

Python: Variable names - Convention (Built-in function names)

Python built-in function names should not be used for variable or function names. These include: abs enumerate list slice aiter eval locals sorted all exec map staticmethod anext filter max str any float memoryview sum ascii format min super bin frozenset next tuple bool getattr object type breakpoint globals oct vars bytearray hasattr open zip bytes hash ord callable help pow chr hex print classmethod id property compile input range complex int repr delattr isinstance reversed dict issubclass round dir iter set divmod len setattr

Using a built-in function name as a variable name can be confusing and may lead to unexpected errors.

Python: Variable names - Convention (Single leading underscores)

Python programmers typically use a single leading underscore to indicate that a variable or function should be treated as private.

A leading underscore name starts with an underscore: _var or _method. Private variables and methods are “internal” to a module. They are not intended for users of a module. Using a leading underscore to indicate something is private is only a convention; it is not part of the language’s syntax.

You should only use a leading underscore when you intend to treat the variable or method as private.

Python: Variable names - Rule (Double leading underscores)

The Python interpreter treats variables beginning with a double underscore specially.

Names with leading double underscores, like __var or __method, will be mangled in some cases. This changes their names internally so they are hard to access within the program. Other names like __file__ or __init__ have special meaning and follow a “magic name” convention.

Avoid using double underscores unless you clearly understand how they work.

Python: Variable names - Best practices

Combining “PEP 8 - Style Guide for Python Code” with the previous rules suggests

Variables and methods should be written in “snake case” – lower-case letters with words separated by underscores: e.g., my_standard_variable (PEP 8) Constant variables (variables that do not change as a program executes) should be written in “screaming snake case” – capitalised letters throughout with words separated by underscores: e.g., MY_CONSTANT_VARIABLE (PEP 8) Do not use reserved keywords or built-in function names for variables. Do not start a variable name with a single underscore unless it is intended to be private. Never start a variable name with two underscores.

Name regular variables in snake_case Name constant variables in SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE Do not use reserved keywords of built-in function names Limit the use of single leading underscores and double leading underscores

Python: An aside about rules and conventions  Rules and conventions

Rules and conventions are important to follow when writing code:

Rules: Define what you are allowed to do Conventions: Define what you should do and, more importantly, what others expect you to have done.

The Python community treats conventions seriously.

“PEP 8 - Style Guide for Python Code” is an essential reference for Python coding conventions.

Rules: Naming

Naming rules apply to any variable or function we create.

Names must start with a letter or underscore (“_“). Names can only contain alphanumeric characters and underscores. Names are case-sensitive.

As long as you follow these rules, you can use nearly anything as a name, however. . .

The Python community has settled on snake_case as the de facto standard when naming variables:

The Python community has settled on snake_case as the de facto standard when naming variables:

It is best to be expressive when naming variables, clearly describing the information to which the variable relates.

Python: Variable names - Rule (Reserved keywords)

Python reserved keywords cannot be used for variable or function names. These include:

Python built-in function names should not be used for variable or function names. These include: abs enumerate list slice aiter eval locals sorted all exec map staticmethod anext filter max str any float memoryview sum ascii format min super bin frozenset next tuple bool getattr object type breakpoint globals oct vars bytearray hasattr open zip bytes hash ord callable help pow chr hex print classmethod id property compile input range complex int repr delattr isinstance reversed dict issubclass round dir iter set divmod len setattr

Using a built-in function name as a variable name can be confusing and may lead to unexpected errors.

Python: Variable names - Convention (Single leading underscores)

Python programmers typically use a single leading underscore to indicate that a variable or function should be treated as private.

A leading underscore name starts with an underscore: _var or _method. Private variables and methods are “internal” to a module. They are not intended for users of a module. Using a leading underscore to indicate something is private is only a convention; it is not part of the language’s syntax.

You should only use a leading underscore when you intend to treat the variable or method as private.

The Python interpreter treats variables beginning with a double underscore specially.

Names with leading double underscores, like __var or __method, will be mangled in some cases. This changes their names internally so they are hard to access within the program. Other names like __file__ or __init__ have special meaning and follow a “magic name” convention.

Avoid using double underscores unless you clearly understand how they work.

Combining “PEP 8 - Style Guide for Python Code” with the previous rules suggests

Variables and methods should be written in “snake case” – lower-case letters with words separated by underscores: e.g., my_standard_variable (PEP 8) Constant variables (variables that do not change as a program executes) should be written in “screaming snake case” – capitalised letters throughout with words separated by underscores: e.g., MY_CONSTANT_VARIABLE (PEP 8) Do not use reserved keywords or built-in function names for variables. Do not start a variable name with a single underscore unless it is intended to be private. Never start a variable name with two underscores.

Name regular variables in snake_case Name constant variables in SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE Do not use reserved keywords of built-in function names Limit the use of single leading underscores and double leading underscores

Review

The Python interpreter creates (and names) many objects by default  These are built-in objects, such as the print function

The Python interpreter creates (and names) many objects by default  These are built-in objects, such as the print function How to check if two objects are the same  id(obj) -> int: Unique identifier for the object  obj1 is obj2: True iff both objects are the same

The Python interpreter creates (and names) many objects by default  These are built-in objects, such as the print function How to check if two objects are the same  id(obj) -> int: Unique identifier for the object  obj1 is obj2: True iff both objects are the same How can I check if two objects have the same value?  obj1 == obj2: True if same value (not necessarily same object) Example: >>> 1 is 1.0 # -> False >>> 1 == 1.0 # -> True

Summary: Assignment statements

A statement is an instruction that the Python interpreter can execute.  In Python, a statement is the smallest standalone unit of execution  Statements often contain expressions  An expression is any code that produces a value (by creating, manipulating, or returning objects) Assignment statement have the form <target> = <expression>  <target> is often a variable name • In that case, the result of <expression> is assigned to <target>

Evaluation steps: 1. Check if <target> is valid (e.g., a valid name) 2. Evaluate <expression>, which produces an object 3. Assign the object produced to <target>

Let’s look at an example

Example: A simple statement

Consider the statement x 1 2

x = 1 + 2 is a valid Python statement

The interpreter evaluates this statement as follows: 1. Check if x is a valid name 2. Evaluate the expression 1 2 step by step: 2.1 The literal 1: Python creates an instance of the class int with value 1. 2.2 The operator +: Python interprets this as an instruction to add two int instances. 2.3 The literal 2: Python creates another int instance with value 2. 2.4 Performing the addition: The class int defines how + works for its instances. Python adds 1 and 2, producing a new int instance with value 3. 3. Assign the result (int: 3) to the variable x

Assignment Statements

Question: Which one is correct?  Objects are assigned to variables, or  Variables are assigned to objects

Assignment Statements

Question: Which one is correct?  Objects are assigned to variables, or  Variables are assigned to objects In this course we will use the wording:  Objects are assigned to variables  This is consistent with the Python Docs That said, both phrasings are acceptable.

Assignment Statements

Question: Which one is correct?  Objects are assigned to variables, or  Variables are assigned to objects In this course we will use the wording:  Objects are assigned to variables  This is consistent with the Python Docs That said, both phrasings are acceptable. What does “x is bound to obj” mean?  It means that the object obj is currently assigned to the variable x

Exercise: Assignment statements

Do not interpret an assignment statement as equality.

Consider: 1 name = "Alice" 2 name = "Bob"

At line 1:  What is the object assigned to name? At line 2:  What is the object assigned name?  Is this the same object as in line 1?

Exercise: Assignment statements

Do not interpret an assignment statement as equality.

Consider: 1 x = 10 2 y = x 3 x = 20

At line 2: At line 3:

Exercise: Assignment statements

Do not interpret an assignment statement as equality.

Consider: 1 x = 1 + 2 2 y = x 3 x = x + 1

At line 1: At line 2:  Are x and y bound to the same object? At line 3:  Are x and y bound to the same object?  An additional note on naming objects

There are multiple ways to name objects. . .

1. Naming an object using assignment statements: [`<variable_name> = <expression>`]{.center}

2. Naming a function, class, or method: def some_func(): ...

Create a function object with name some_func 3. Naming modules with import statements: import some_module

Create a module object with name some_module

We will discuss functions and modules in the coming weeks.

Discussion: Downloading stock prices  Calling functions

To call a function named some_func:  Without parameters: some_func()  With parameters: some_func(parm1, parm2=2)  A function specifies how many parameters it takes, their names, and whether they are required  Use help(some_func) for more information about some_func

Calling functions

To call a function named some_func:  Without parameters: some_func()  With parameters: some_func(parm1, parm2=2)  A function specifies how many parameters it takes, their names, and whether they are required  Use help(some_func) for more information about some_func Think of functions as “recipes”  The “ingredients” are called parameters  Calling a function runs the recipe We will discuss how to create functions later in the course

Calling classes

To call a class named some_class:  Without parameters: some_class()  With parameters: some_class(parm1, parm2=2)  A class specifies how many parameters it takes, their names, and whether they are required

Calling classes

To call a class named some_class:  Without parameters: some_class()  With parameters: some_class(parm1, parm2=2)  A class specifies how many parameters it takes, their names, and whether they are required Think of classes as factories of instances  The “ingredients” are called parameters, which are specific to the instance  Calling a class creates an instance of that type

Calling classes

To call a class named some_class:  Without parameters: some_class()  With parameters: some_class(parm1, parm2=2)  A class specifies how many parameters it takes, their names, and whether they are required Think of classes as factories of instances  The “ingredients” are called parameters, which are specific to the instance  Calling a class creates an instance of that type A class can define its own “recipes”  These are called methods instead of functions  They automatically take the instance itself as a parameter

Let’s look at a few examples in PyCharm

What about using Python instead?

What about using Python instead? One approach would be to write a function, for instance:  Create a function called get_history which takes one parameter called ticker  In principle, calling get_history(ticker='AAPL') would download historical prices for Apple

What about using Python instead? One approach would be to write a function, for instance:  Create a function called get_history which takes one parameter called ticker  In principle, calling get_history(ticker='AAPL') would download historical prices for Apple Another approach would be to create a class called Ticker  This class defines attributes (such as the ticker) and methods (such as get_history)  Calling this class creates an instance of the Ticker type >>> type(tic) <class 'Ticker'>

 Calling the instance method tic.get_history() would download historical prices Let us understand the second approach using UML

It defines an attribute (also called ticker) to hold the “value” of the ticker

It defines an attribute (also called ticker) to hold the “value” of the ticker It defines a method called get_history  When called, this method will download prices from YahooFinance  Importantly, we do not pass a parameter to the get_ticker method • The class will use the value of the ticker attribute instead

It defines an attribute (also called ticker) to hold the “value” of the ticker It defines a method called get_history  When called, this method will download prices from YahooFinance  Importantly, we do not pass a parameter to the get_ticker method • The class will use the value of the ticker attribute instead

We can represent this class using the UML diagrams discussed in the videos The Ticker class

Ticker

.ticker: str

.get_history(): None

Note that: The attribute ticker will hold values of the str type The method get_history does not take any parameters and returns None

The Ticker instance

The class The instance

Ticker tic: Ticker

.ticker: str .ticker: str = ‘AAPL’

.get_history(): None .get_history(): None

The class defined an attribute called ticker  In the instance, the value of this attribute is 'AAPL' The class defined a method called get_history  So, this method is available to the instance We could access both the attribute and method using “dot” notation  NOTE: Do not run the code below yet (we will do it later)

>>> tic.ticker AAPL

>>> type(tic.ticker) <class 'str'>

>>> tic.get_history <bound method Ticker.get_history of <Ticker object at ...>>

>>> type(tic.get_history) <class 'method'>  Calling the tic.get_history method

The class The instance

Ticker tic: Ticker

.ticker: str .ticker: str = ‘AAPL’

.get_history(): None .get_history(): None

The behaviour of get_history is defined by the class Suppose this is what happens when tic.get_history is called 1. Connect to YahooFinance somehow 2. Download historical prices for the ticker in tic.ticker

In that case, the answer may not be so obvious. . . Good news is that you can choose whichever approach you like  Using functions only, classes only, or a combination IMPORTANT:  In this course, we will only write functions, not classes  But we will use classes written by other people • E.g., the Ticker class defined inside the yfinance module

But what is a module?

 Instead of writing everything from scratch, we can import code from existing modules

 Instead of writing everything from scratch, we can import code from existing modules A Python module can be:  A single file with a .py extension  A folder containing an __init__.py file • This folder may also include multiple .py files • Such modules are called packages

 Instead of writing everything from scratch, we can import code from existing modules A Python module can be:  A single file with a .py extension  A folder containing an __init__.py file • This folder may also include multiple .py files • Such modules are called packages

The tk_utils/setup.py script installed many useful modules  These are not part of Python’s standard library  Examples: numpy, pandas, yfinance, and others

 Instead of writing everything from scratch, we can import code from existing modules A Python module can be:  A single file with a .py extension  A folder containing an __init__.py file • This folder may also include multiple .py files • Such modules are called packages

The tk_utils/setup.py script installed many useful modules  These are not part of Python’s standard library  Examples: numpy, pandas, yfinance, and others Today, we will take our first look at modules and how to import them

We will discuss modules in more detail later in the course

To access this functionality, you need to import the module first >>> yfinance <module 'yfinance' from '...toolkit/.venv/.../yfinance/__init__.py'>

This module defines a class called Ticker  During import, Python created a class object and named it Ticker

To access this functionality, you need to import the module first >>> yfinance <module 'yfinance' from '...toolkit/.venv/.../yfinance/__init__.py'>

This module defines a class called Ticker  During import, Python created a class object and named it Ticker The following will not work >>> Ticker ... NameError: name 'Ticker' is not defined

This is because the name “Ticker” is only available inside the yfinance module

To access this functionality, you need to import the module first >>> yfinance <module 'yfinance' from '...toolkit/.venv/.../yfinance/__init__.py'>

This module defines a class called Ticker  During import, Python created a class object and named it Ticker The following will not work >>> Ticker ... NameError: name 'Ticker' is not defined

This is because the name “Ticker” is only available inside the yfinance module To access names defined inside modules, we use “dot” notation >>> yfinance.Ticker <class 'yfinance.ticker.Ticker'>

This tells Python to search for the name Ticker inside the yfinance module  Note that Ticker is bound to a class called Ticker  The yfinance.Ticker class

The yfinance.Ticker class defines lots of attributes and methods  Use the help function to learn more about this class >>> help(yfinance.Ticker)

The yfinance.Ticker class

The yfinance.Ticker class defines lots of attributes and methods  Use the help function to learn more about this class >>> help(yfinance.Ticker)

The method we want is called history  This method is available to any instance of yfinance.Ticker >>> tic = yfinance.Ticker('aapl') >>> tic.history()

The code above will show you the most recent prices for Apple
